perturbation.original_text: 4.3.1 In conducting the Valeant Activities hereunder, Valeant shall, and shall require all Field Force Personnel to, comply in all respects with Applicable Laws. In addition, Dova shall, and shall require all of its sales representatives to, comply in all respects with Applicable Laws in connection with its promotion of the Product in the Territory.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3.1 In conducting the Valeant Activities hereunder, Valeant shall use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure all Field Force Personnel comply with Applicable Laws. In addition, Dova will encourage its sales representatives to comply with Applicable Laws in connection with its promotion of the Product in the Territory.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that both Valeant and Dova *shall* require their personnel to comply in all respects with Applicable Laws. The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall comply in all respects' to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure comply' and 'encourage ... to comply.' This weakens the obligation, as 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'encourage' are subjective standards open to interpretation and do not guarantee full compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the strict requirements of the Anti-Kickback Statute, which prohibits offering, paying, soliciting, or receiving remuneration to induce referrals for services reimbursable by federal healthcare programs. The 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'encourage' standards could be interpreted as allowing activities that skirt the edges of prohibited conduct, failing to ensure the absolute compliance required by the statute, particularly concerning inducements and remuneration.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates strict compliance with applicable laws by Valeant and Dova personnel. The modified text weakens this obligation by changing 'shall comply in all respects' to 'use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure comply' and 'encourage ... to comply,' introducing ambiguity and potentially allowing for non-compliance. Although the scraped snippets are not directly usable, the law explanation specifically mentions the Anti-Kickback Statute, which requires strict adherence and prohibits remuneration to induce referrals. The change creates a legal contradiction because 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'encourage' are subjective and may not guarantee the absolute compliance required by the statute, making the obligation difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3.10 If, during the Term, Valeant becomes aware of a material violation or failure to comply with Applicable Law or the terms of this Agreement by a member of the Field Force Personnel that are engaged in Detailing, it shall promptly, but no later than two (2) Business Days after it becomes aware, notify Dova of such violation and, as promptly as possible thereafter, shall notify the steps it has taken or intends to take to remediate such violation.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3.10 If, during the Term, Valeant suspects a possible violation or failure to substantially comply with Applicable Law or the terms of this Agreement by a member of the Field Force Personnel that are engaged in Detailing, it should consider notifying Dova of such potential violation when deemed appropriate and, if feasible, notify the general steps it may consider taking to address such a potential violation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear obligation for Valeant to notify Dova of material violations within two business days. The modified text introduces ambiguity by changing 'becomes aware of a material violation' to 'suspects a possible violation,' 'shall promptly' to 'should consider,' 'no later than two (2) Business Days' to 'when deemed appropriate,' and 'shall notify the steps it has taken or intends to take to remediate' to 'notify the general steps it may consider taking.' These changes make the notification requirement discretionary and vague, undermining the original intent of timely reporting and remediation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Physician Payments Sunshine Act (Open Payments)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's discretionary language regarding notification conflicts with the intent and potentially the requirements of the Physician Payments Sunshine Act. This act mandates the reporting of payments and other transfers of value to physicians and teaching hospitals. If Valeant only 'considers' reporting 'possible' violations 'when deemed appropriate,' potential violations of the Anti-Kickback Statute or False Claims Act may go unreported, leading to inaccurate or incomplete data under the Sunshine Act. This is because potential inducements or kickbacks, which are not promptly reported and remediated, could lead to improper payments that must be disclosed under the Sunshine Act. Delayed or non-existent reporting obscures transparency and accountability, undermining the goals of the Sunshine Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion, which contradicts the intent of the Physician Payments Sunshine Act as described in the Law Explanation. The original text requires prompt notification of material violations, while the modified text makes notification discretionary ('should consider') and vague ('when deemed appropriate'). This directly undermines the Act's goal of transparency and accountability by potentially allowing violations to go unreported. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly define the Act, the Law Explanation highlights that the discretionary language conflicts with the reporting mandates related to potential violations of the Anti-Kickback Statute or False Claims Act, which are relevant under the Sunshine Act.

perturbation.original_text: 5.7 Certain Reporting Responsibilities. Notwithstanding the foregoing provisions of this ARTICLE 5, each Party shall be responsible for its own federal, state and local government pricing reporting and payment transparency reporting in the Territory arising from its Product promotional activities and related expenditures pursuant to Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.7 Certain Reporting Responsibilities. Notwithstanding the foregoing provisions of this ARTICLE 5, each Party should generally endeavor to handle its own federal, state and local government pricing reporting and payment transparency reporting in the Territory potentially arising from its Product promotional activities and possibly related expenditures as it determines are advisable under Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be responsible' to 'should generally endeavor to handle,' 'arising from' to 'potentially arising from', 'related expenditures pursuant to Applicable Law' to 'possibly related expenditures as it determines are advisable under Applicable Law' the modified text makes the obligation to report pricing and payment transparency far less definitive. This weakens the original clear obligation and introduces subjectivity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Drug Price Competition and Patent Term Restoration Act (Hatch-Waxman Amendments)
perturbation.law_explanation: The weakened reporting obligation could contradict the Hatch-Waxman Amendments, particularly concerning accurate reporting of drug pricing information which is critical for generic drug approvals and market competition. Under the Hatch-Waxman Act, brand-name drug companies must accurately list patents related to their approved drugs in the FDA's Orange Book. This information, along with accurate pricing data, is used to determine when generic versions can enter the market. If companies only 'endeavor to handle' pricing reporting 'as it determines are advisable', the potential for misreporting or incomplete reporting increases. This could delay or impede the entry of generic drugs, undermining the intent of the Hatch-Waxman Amendments to promote competition and lower drug costs. Inaccurate or delayed reporting of pricing information can obstruct the process by which generic drug manufacturers rely on accurate data to challenge patents and obtain approval for their generic versions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Laws Enforced by FDA Home Regulatory Information Laws Enforced by FDA Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act) Laws Enforced by FDA FD&C Act Reference Information United States Code, Title 21 The laws of the United States are organized by subject into the United States Code. The United States Code contains only the currently enacted statutory language. The official United States Code is maintained by the Office of the Law Revision Counsel in the United States House of Representatives. The Office of the Law Revision Counsel reviews enacted laws and determines where the statutory language should be codified related to its topic. The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and subsequent amending statutes are codified into Title 21 Chapter 9 of the United States Code. The listing of FD&C Act sections presented here identifies both the FD&C Act and U.S. Code section numbers, which can be used to narrow your search on the Law Revision Counsel website. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, use the Advanced Search to limit the your search to Title 21. To search the FD&C Act on the Law Revision Counsel website, you may either search by U.S. code section number or browse the Title 21 section listing . Content current as of: 03/29/2018 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be responsible' to 'should generally endeavor to handle' significantly weakens the obligation for accurate reporting, creating ambiguity. The law explanation suggests that this ambiguity could undermine the Hatch-Waxman Amendments related to drug pricing accuracy, which is used for generic drug approvals. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act), indicating the existence of laws and regulations concerning drug information, which the modified text potentially contradicts. The altered text introduces subjectivity and reduces accountability, making compliance less certain and potentially non-compliant.
